title: Watermark making with wire
subtitle: Stitching a wire design onto a papermaking mould to create a translucent mark in the sheet
excerpt: Watermarks have been stitched onto papermaking moulds since the thirteenth century. The wire image sits on the mould surface; the wet fibres form more thinly over the raised wire, creating a translucent motif visible when the dry sheet is held to the light. Making a simple wire watermark requires no special tools beyond fine wire and a needle.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain), chapter on watermarks and mould construction. Internet Archive scan available. Hunter describes wire watermark attachment methods in detail.

glossaryTerms.definition: A translucent image or text in a sheet of paper, formed by areas of reduced fibre thickness corresponding to a raised wire design on the papermaking mould.
The closely spaced parallel wires of a laid mould surface, visible in a finished sheet held to the light as a pattern of fine lines.
The widely spaced wires running perpendicular to the laid lines on a laid mould, used to attach the laid wires to the mould frame.

content.text: A
watermark

is made by stitching a shaped wire onto the surface of a papermaking mould. Where the wire raises the mould surface, fewer fibres settle during pulling, creating a thinner zone in the final sheet. The simplest wire watermarks are geometric: a circle, a diamond, an initial letter. Complex portraits and intricate heraldic designs are the work of specialist wireworkers, but a simple design is achievable by any papermaker.
Keep the vat blender unplugged when adding fibre. Drain the vat before reaching in with your hands.
What you need
Wire: fine brass or stainless steel wire, approximately 0.4 to 0.5 mm diameter. Softer wire bends more easily into curves; stiffer wire holds its shape better during production.
Mould: a small A5 laid or wove mould. A wove mould (mesh surface) shows a cleaner watermark because the background is even; a laid mould has the
laid-lines
and
chain-lines
visible as a background pattern behind the watermark.
Attachment wire: finer wire (0.2 mm) or strong thread for stitching the design wire to the mould surface.
Making and attaching the wire design
Draw the design on paper at the size required. Keep it simple: a circle 40 mm in diameter, a square, or an initial letter as a single continuous line where possible.
Lay the wire over the paper template and bend it to shape with small pliers or fingers. For curves, bend the wire gently around a dowel of the appropriate diameter.
Position the shaped wire on the mould surface where the watermark should appear (usually centred or in the lower third of the sheet).
Stitch the wire to the mould with the fine attachment wire, threading it through the mould mesh at 10 mm intervals around the design. Twist the ends together on the underside and clip off.
Pull several test sheets and check the watermark by holding the wet sheet up to the light. If the mark is too faint, the design wire is too light; try a heavier gauge.
Troubleshooting

attrs.symptom: Watermark is not visible in the dry sheet
attrs.cause: The furnish was too thick, obscuring the thinning effect of the wire.

content.text: Use a thinner furnish (less fibre per litre of vat water) for watermark work. The sheet should be very thin to show the mark clearly.

attrs.symptom: The design wire lifts away from the mould during sheet-pulling
attrs.cause: Insufficient stitching points; the wire has lifted at an unsecured curve.

content.text: Add attachment stitches at every 5 mm on curves and at every corner. Curved sections need more stitching than straight sections.
